explanation: The phrase 'If prop_0, prop_1.' expresses that whenever prop_0 is true, prop_1 must also be true. This is a safety property and can be represented by globally (G), stating that at all times, if prop_0 holds then prop_1 must also hold.

The statement 'If prop_0, prop_1.' is interpreted as 'whenever prop_0 holds, prop_1 must also hold at the same time.' This is a simple implication that must apply at every moment in the temporal model, so we use the 'globally' (G) operator with an implication from prop_0 to prop_1.

The sentence expresses a conditional: whenever prop_0 happens during inspiration, then prop_1 should hold. In temporal logic, this is generally translated as: globally, if prop_0 holds, then prop_1 must also hold. We're not given an explicit 'inspiration' proposition (unless 'inspiration' is subsumed in prop_0), but the given phrasing suggests we should focus on the implication aspect.
This statement expresses that whenever prop_0 holds, then prop_1 must also hold at the same time. This is a safety property expressed globally.
This translates to: if prop_0 holds at any time, then prop_1 must also hold at that time. So at every point, if prop_0 is true, then prop_1 must be true.

The natural language sentence 'If prop_0, prop_1.' expresses a conditional: whenever prop_0 holds, prop_1 must also hold. In LTL, this is usually captured globally, so at all times, if prop_0 is true, then prop_1 must also be true.

The specification states that whenever 'prop_0' is true, 'prop_1' must also be true at that same position. In LTL, this is typically expressed globally: at every point, either 'prop_0' is false or 'prop_1' is true.

The statement says that whenever 'prop_0' holds, so should 'prop_1'. Globally, if prop_0 happens at any time, then at the same time, prop_1 must also be true. This is captured by 'G(prop_0 -> prop_1)'.

This statement expresses that if 'prop_0' holds, then 'prop_1' must also hold at the same time and point. In LTL, this is captured by the implication: whenever 'prop_0' holds, 'prop_1' must hold as well.
This expression means that whenever prop_0 holds, then prop_1 must also hold at that moment. In LTL, this is captured by stating it is always true that if prop_0 is true, then prop_1 is also true.
